---
title: "High-Dimensional Statistics"
description: "Sparsity, regularization, high-dimensional regression, covariance estimation, minimax limits, and inference when the ambient dimension is large relative to sample size."
page-type: module-overview
status: published
update-status: current
level: advanced
topic: high-dimensional-statistics
prerequisites:
  - /topics/foundation/statistics/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/optimization/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/matrix-analysis/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/high-dimensional-probability/index.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Understand why high-dimensional statistics needs structural assumptions such as sparsity and low complexity."
  - "Know the intended first-pass order for the high-dimensional-statistics module."
  - "Connect regularization, geometry, random matrices, and statistical guarantees in the p >> n regime."
applications:
  - machine-learning-theory
  - high-dimensional-regression
  - sparse-recovery
keywords:
  - high-dimensional statistics
  - sparsity
  - lasso
  - compressed sensing
  - minimax
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-25
source-check-date: 2026-04-25
review-cadence: annual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
stable-sources:
  - "Stanford high-dimensional statistics research page"
  - "Stanford STATS 202 high-dimensional regression notes"
  - "Berkeley EECS 208 course site"
current-sources:
  - "Stanford high-dimensional statistics page"
  - "Stanford STATS 202 notes"
  - "Stanford STATS 305B LASSO notes"
  - "Berkeley EECS 208 course site"
  - "CMU 36-709 syllabus"
---

## Why This Module Matters

Classical statistics usually imagines that sample size is comfortably larger than the number of parameters.

High-dimensional statistics studies the regime where that intuition breaks:

- the number of features can be comparable to or much larger than sample size
- naive least squares can become non-unique or unstable
- recovery becomes possible only with additional structure
- guarantees depend on geometry, concentration, and regularization all at once

That is why modern papers keep talking about:

- sparsity
- shrinkage
- restricted eigenvalues
- random design
- minimax lower bounds
- inference after selection or regularization

This module is the bridge from ordinary estimation language to the research-facing world of `p >> n`, sparse recovery, and high-dimensional inference.

::: {.meta-strip}
::: {.meta-box}
**Prerequisites**
[Statistics](../../foundation/statistics/index.qmd) should come first. [Optimization](../optimization/index.qmd) matters because many estimators are posed as penalized optimization problems. [Matrix Analysis](../matrix-analysis/index.qmd) and [High-Dimensional Probability](../high-dimensional-probability/index.qmd) become important as soon as spectral control, random design, and concentration enter the story.
:::
::: {.meta-box}
**Unlocks**
Sparse regression, compressed sensing, covariance estimation, minimax arguments, post-selection and debiased inference
:::
::: {.meta-box}
**Research Use**
Reading papers on lasso, high-dimensional regression, sparse recovery, covariance/PCA, uncertainty in large-feature regimes, and modern statistical limits
:::
:::

## First Pass Through This Module

The intended first-pass spine for this module is:

1. [Sparsity and Regularization](concepts/sparsity-and-regularization.qmd)
2. [Lasso and Compressed Sensing Basics](concepts/lasso-and-compressed-sensing-basics.qmd)
3. [Design Geometry: Restricted Eigenvalues, Coherence, and RIP](concepts/design-geometry-restricted-eigenvalues-coherence-and-rip.qmd)
4. [High-Dimensional Regression](concepts/high-dimensional-regression.qmd)
5. [Covariance, PCA, and Spectral Estimation in High Dimension](concepts/covariance-pca-and-spectral-estimation-in-high-dimension.qmd)
6. [Minimax and Lower Bounds](concepts/minimax-and-lower-bounds.qmd)
7. [Inference in High Dimension](concepts/inference-in-high-dimension.qmd)

The module now opens with a complete seven-page first-pass spine, moving from ill-posedness and regularization to sparse estimators, then to the geometry conditions that make sparse theorems work, and then onward to regression rates, covariance/PCA, minimax limits, and inference after selection or shrinkage.

## How To Use This Module

Read this module in spine order.

For a clean first pass, that means:

1. start with [Sparsity and Regularization](concepts/sparsity-and-regularization.qmd)
2. continue to [Lasso and Compressed Sensing Basics](concepts/lasso-and-compressed-sensing-basics.qmd)
3. continue to [Design Geometry: Restricted Eigenvalues, Coherence, and RIP](concepts/design-geometry-restricted-eigenvalues-coherence-and-rip.qmd)
4. continue to [High-Dimensional Regression](concepts/high-dimensional-regression.qmd)
5. continue to [Covariance, PCA, and Spectral Estimation in High Dimension](concepts/covariance-pca-and-spectral-estimation-in-high-dimension.qmd)
6. continue to [Minimax and Lower Bounds](concepts/minimax-and-lower-bounds.qmd)
7. continue to [Inference in High Dimension](concepts/inference-in-high-dimension.qmd)
8. then use nearby live pages in [Statistics](../../foundation/statistics/index.qmd), [Optimization](../optimization/index.qmd), [Learning Theory](../learning-theory/index.qmd), [Matrix Analysis](../matrix-analysis/index.qmd), and [High-Dimensional Probability](../high-dimensional-probability/index.qmd) whenever a page talks about concentration, operator control, or estimation geometry

This module should stay focused on the structural ideas that make estimation possible in large-feature regimes, rather than trying to become a full statistical-learning textbook.

## Core Concepts

- [Sparsity and Regularization](concepts/sparsity-and-regularization.qmd): the opening page that explains why high-dimensional problems are ill-posed without structural assumptions and why regularization is the first remedy.
- [Lasso and Compressed Sensing Basics](concepts/lasso-and-compressed-sensing-basics.qmd): the page that turns sparsity into concrete convex estimators and sparse recovery guarantees.
- [Design Geometry: Restricted Eigenvalues, Coherence, and RIP](concepts/design-geometry-restricted-eigenvalues-coherence-and-rip.qmd): the page that explains which matrix-geometry conditions sparse-recovery theorems are really asking for.
- [High-Dimensional Regression](concepts/high-dimensional-regression.qmd): the page that connects prediction error, estimation error, support recovery, and random-design geometry.
- [Covariance, PCA, and Spectral Estimation in High Dimension](concepts/covariance-pca-and-spectral-estimation-in-high-dimension.qmd): the page that turns covariance and PCA into explicit random-matrix and eigenspace-estimation problems.
- [Minimax and Lower Bounds](concepts/minimax-and-lower-bounds.qmd): the page that explains what rates are fundamentally possible and what no method can beat over a model class.
- [Inference in High Dimension](concepts/inference-in-high-dimension.qmd): the page that explains why confidence intervals and p-values become delicate after selection or regularization, and how debiasing or selective inference try to repair that.

## Proof Patterns In This Module

- `Structure defeats ill-posedness`: identify which assumption makes recovery or estimation possible.
- `Geometry plus concentration`: combine random design control with matrix or norm inequalities.
- `Upper bound versus lower bound`: separate what an estimator achieves from what the problem fundamentally allows.

## Applications

### Sparse Regression And Recovery

Many modern regression problems only become statistically meaningful when the model is sparse, approximately sparse, or otherwise low-complexity.

### Spectral Estimation

Covariance estimation, PCA, and related matrix problems depend on the same operator and concentration language already built elsewhere on the site.

### ML Theory And Modern Regimes

High-dimensional statistics gives concrete versions of questions that also appear in ML theory: overparameterization, shrinkage, stability, bias, variance, and recoverability.

## Go Deeper By Topic

The strongest adjacent live pages right now are:

- [Statistics](../../foundation/statistics/index.qmd)
- [Optimization](../optimization/index.qmd)
- [Learning Theory](../learning-theory/index.qmd)
- [High-Dimensional Probability](../high-dimensional-probability/index.qmd)
- [Regularization, Implicit Bias, and Model Complexity](../../../applications/machine-learning/regularization-implicit-bias-and-model-complexity.qmd)

## Optional Deeper Reading After First Pass

The strongest current references connected to this module are:

- [Stanford high-dimensional statistics](https://statistics.stanford.edu/research/high-dimensional-statistics) - official current research page showing the field and its main themes. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford STATS 202: High-dimensional regression](https://web.stanford.edu/class/stats202/notes/Model-selection/High-dimensional.html) - official notes for the p >> n regime and regularization viewpoint. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford STATS 305B: LASSO](https://web.stanford.edu/class/stats305b/notes/LASSO_I.html) - official notes for lasso and sparsity-inducing penalties. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Berkeley EECS 208](https://pages.github.berkeley.edu/UCB-EECS208/course_site/) - official course site for high-dimensional signal and data analysis. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [CMU 36-709 syllabus](https://www.stat.cmu.edu/~siva/teaching/709current/syllabus.pdf) - official syllabus for advanced non-asymptotic theoretical statistics. Checked `2026-04-25`.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [Stanford high-dimensional statistics](https://statistics.stanford.edu/research/high-dimensional-statistics) - `First pass` - official current page showing the field and its main research themes. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford STATS 202: High-dimensional regression](https://web.stanford.edu/class/stats202/notes/Model-selection/High-dimensional.html) - `First pass` - official notes for the p >> n mindset, regularization, and interpretation issues. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford STATS 305B: LASSO](https://web.stanford.edu/class/stats305b/notes/LASSO_I.html) - `First pass` - official notes on lasso, penalties, and sparse estimation. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Berkeley EECS 208](https://pages.github.berkeley.edu/UCB-EECS208/course_site/) - `Second pass` - official course site connecting high-dimensional geometry, recovery, and applications. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [CMU 36-709 syllabus](https://www.stat.cmu.edu/~siva/teaching/709current/syllabus.pdf) - `Second pass` - official syllabus showing a broader advanced theoretical-statistics route into the area. Checked `2026-04-25`.
